subject: Constitutional Law
coverage_standard: NCBE-aligned Constitutional Law rule bank built around the MBE outline categories: judicial review and justiciability, separation of powers, federalism, individual rights, First Amendment, due process, equal protection, and takings

rules.title: Judicial Review
rules.prompt_question: What is the rule of judicial review?
rules.rule_statement: The Supreme Court has authority to review the constitutionality of acts of Congress and executive actions and may invalidate governmental action that conflicts with the Constitution.
rules.application_example: If Congress passes a statute that exceeds an enumerated power, a federal court may declare it unconstitutional.
rules.common_trap: Judicial review does not permit federal courts to issue advisory opinions.

rules.title: Case or Controversy Requirement
rules.prompt_question: What is the case or controversy requirement?
rules.rule_statement: Federal courts may decide only actual, ongoing cases or controversies and may not issue advisory opinions.
rules.application_example: A federal court may not answer a purely hypothetical constitutional question.
rules.common_trap: This requirement underlies standing, ripeness, mootness, and the ban on advisory opinions.

rules.title: Standing
rules.prompt_question: What are the constitutional requirements for standing?
rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff must show a concrete and particularized injury in fact, causation, and redressability.
rules.application_example: A taxpayer generally lacks standing to challenge ordinary federal spending because the injury is too generalized.
rules.common_trap: Standing must exist when the suit is filed.

rules.title: Third Party Standing
rules.prompt_question: When may a party assert the rights of others?
rules.rule_statement: A litigant generally may not raise the rights of third parties, but may do so if there is a close relationship and a hindrance to the third party's ability to protect their own interests.
rules.application_example: A doctor may sometimes assert the rights of patients seeking abortions.
rules.common_trap: This is an exception to the general rule against third party standing.

rules.title: Associational Standing
rules.prompt_question: When does an organization have associational standing?
rules.rule_statement: An association may sue on behalf of its members when members would have standing, the interests are germane to the organization's purpose, and neither the claim nor the relief requires individual member participation.
rules.application_example: A trade association may challenge a regulation harming its members if individualized damages proof is not required.
rules.common_trap: Associational standing is harder when individualized monetary damages are sought.

rules.title: Taxpayer Standing
rules.prompt_question: When does a taxpayer have standing?
rules.rule_statement: A taxpayer generally lacks standing to challenge government expenditures, except a federal taxpayer may challenge a congressional spending program alleged to violate the Establishment Clause under the narrow Flast exception.
rules.application_example: A federal taxpayer may challenge congressional spending that funds religious activity in violation of the Establishment Clause.
rules.common_trap: The exception is narrow and does not apply to executive spending decisions or most constitutional claims.

rules.title: Ripeness
rules.prompt_question: When is a claim ripe?
rules.rule_statement: A claim is ripe when there is a present, concrete dispute and withholding review would create hardship, especially where further factual development is unnecessary.
rules.application_example: A pre-enforcement challenge to a law threatening imminent penalties may be ripe.
rules.common_trap: Ripeness often matters in pre-enforcement suits.

rules.title: Mootness
rules.prompt_question: When is a case moot?
rules.rule_statement: A case is moot when the issues are no longer live or the parties lack a legally cognizable interest in the outcome.
rules.application_example: If the challenged policy is repealed and no ongoing injury remains, the case may be moot.
rules.common_trap: There are important exceptions, including conduct capable of repetition yet evading review.

rules.title: Exceptions to Mootness
rules.prompt_question: What are the main exceptions to mootness?
rules.rule_statement: A case may avoid mootness if the issue is capable of repetition yet evading review, the defendant voluntarily ceases the conduct but may resume it, a class action remains live, or collateral consequences persist.
keywords: capable of repetition yet evading review
rules.application_example: An election-law challenge may survive mootness if the issue is likely to recur but too short in duration to be fully litigated each time.
rules.common_trap: Voluntary cessation by the government does not always moot a case.

rules.title: Political Question Doctrine
rules.prompt_question: When is an issue a nonjusticiable political question?
rules.rule_statement: A federal court will not decide an issue committed by the Constitution to another branch or lacking judicially manageable standards for resolution.
rules.application_example: Challenges to partisan gerrymandering are treated as political questions in federal court.
rules.common_trap: Not every case touching politics is a political question.

rules.title: Adequate and Independent State Ground
rules.prompt_question: When may the Supreme Court review a state court judgment?
rules.rule_statement: The Supreme Court may not review a state court judgment resting on an adequate and independent state law ground sufficient to support the result.
rules.application_example: If a state supreme court decision rests on a separate state constitutional rule that independently supports the judgment, Supreme Court review is barred.
rules.common_trap: If it is unclear whether the state ground is independent, the Court may review.

rules.title: Eleventh Amendment Sovereign Immunity
rules.prompt_question: What does the Eleventh Amendment bar?
rules.rule_statement: The Eleventh Amendment generally bars suits in federal court against a state by private parties for damages or retroactive relief, absent consent, valid congressional abrogation under Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment, or certain other exceptions.
rules.application_example: A citizen generally may not sue a state in federal court for money damages under a federal statute unless immunity was validly abrogated.
rules.common_trap: Congress cannot abrogate state sovereign immunity under Article I powers alone.

rules.title: Ex parte Young Exception
rules.prompt_question: What is the Ex parte Young exception?
rules.rule_statement: A plaintiff may sue a state officer in federal court for prospective injunctive relief to stop an ongoing violation of federal law.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff may seek an injunction against a state attorney general enforcing an unconstitutional statute.
rules.common_trap: This exception does not allow retroactive monetary relief payable from the state treasury.

rules.title: State Officers and Personal Liability
rules.prompt_question: May state officers be sued personally?
rules.rule_statement: State officers may be sued in their personal capacity for damages, subject to personal immunities, because such suits are not treated as suits against the state.
rules.application_example: A state officer may face personal Section 1983 liability for unconstitutional conduct.
rules.common_trap: Official-capacity damages suits are generally barred as suits against the state.

rules.title: Necessary and Proper Clause
rules.prompt_question: What power does the Necessary and Proper Clause give Congress?
rules.rule_statement: Congress may use any means rationally related to the implementation of an enumerated power, so long as the means are not prohibited by the Constitution.
rules.application_example: Congress may create a federal bank as a means of carrying out fiscal powers.
rules.common_trap: The clause is not an independent source of power; it supports other enumerated powers.

rules.title: Commerce Clause Categories
rules.prompt_question: What may Congress regulate under the Commerce Clause?
rules.rule_statement: Congress may regulate the channels of interstate commerce, the instrumentalities of interstate commerce, and activities that have a substantial effect on interstate commerce.
rules.application_example: Congress may regulate railroads and goods moving across state lines.
rules.common_trap: Purely noneconomic local activity is harder to justify under the substantial-effects category.

rules.title: Substantial Effects on Interstate Commerce
rules.prompt_question: When may Congress regulate local activity under the Commerce Clause?
rules.rule_statement: Congress may regulate economic or commercial intrastate activity that, in the aggregate, substantially affects interstate commerce.
rules.application_example: Congress may regulate local production of wheat grown for home consumption because of aggregate economic effects.
rules.common_trap: The Court is less deferential when the regulated activity is noneconomic.

rules.title: Limits on Commerce Power Over Noneconomic Activity
rules.prompt_question: What is the limit on Congress regulating noneconomic activity?
rules.rule_statement: Congress generally may not regulate noneconomic intrastate activity based solely on attenuated effects on interstate commerce, unless the regulation is part of a broader regulatory scheme and the activity is essential to that scheme.
rules.application_example: A law criminalizing possession near schools cannot be sustained merely by cumulative effects on commerce.
rules.common_trap: Be careful to distinguish economic regulation from noneconomic conduct.

rules.title: Taxing Power
rules.prompt_question: What is Congress's taxing power?
rules.rule_statement: Congress may tax and spend for the general welfare, and a tax is generally valid if it raises some revenue and is not a penalty in disguise prohibited by some other constitutional provision.
rules.application_example: Congress may impose a tax on certain transactions if it functions as a tax and not an unconstitutional command.
rules.common_trap: A tax tied to unconstitutional discrimination may still fail.

rules.title: Spending Power and Conditional Grants
rules.prompt_question: When may Congress attach conditions to federal funds?
rules.rule_statement: Congress may condition federal grants on compliance with federal requirements if the spending is for the general welfare, conditions are unambiguous, related to the program, not independently unconstitutional, and not unduly coercive.
rules.application_example: Congress may attach highway funding conditions related to highway safety.
rules.common_trap: A condition can be unconstitutional if coercive enough to compel state participation.

rules.title: War and Related Powers
rules.prompt_question: What war-related powers does Congress possess?
rules.rule_statement: Congress has powers to declare war, raise and support armies, provide for the navy, call forth and regulate the militia, and make rules concerning captures and military governance.
rules.application_example: Congress may authorize military funding and regulate armed forces.
rules.common_trap: Foreign affairs often involve overlapping executive authority.

rules.title: Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment
rules.prompt_question: What power does Congress have under Section 5 of the Fourteenth Amendment?
rules.rule_statement: Congress may enforce the Fourteenth Amendment by adopting legislation that is congruent and proportional to constitutional violations by state actors.
rules.application_example: Congress may provide remedies targeting state violations of equal protection if the statute is appropriately tailored.
rules.common_trap: Section 5 allows enforcement, not redefinition, of constitutional rights.

rules.title: Property and Territorial Powers
rules.prompt_question: What power does Congress have over federal property and territories?
rules.rule_statement: Congress has plenary power over federal property and territories and may regulate and dispose of them.
rules.application_example: Congress may regulate conduct on federal lands.
rules.common_trap: This is separate from the Commerce Clause.

rules.title: Anti-Commandeering Principle
rules.prompt_question: What is the anti-commandeering principle?
rules.rule_statement: Congress may not compel state legislatures or state executive officials to enact, administer, or enforce a federal regulatory program.
rules.application_example: Congress may not require state officers to conduct federal background checks.
rules.common_trap: Congress may regulate private actors directly and may encourage state participation through valid spending conditions.

rules.title: Preemption
rules.prompt_question: When does federal law preempt state law?
rules.rule_statement: Federal law preempts conflicting state law under the Supremacy Clause by express preemption, field preemption, or conflict preemption where compliance with both laws is impossible or state law obstructs federal objectives.
rules.application_example: A state law conflicting with federal immigration law may be preempted.
rules.common_trap: There is often a presumption against preemption in areas of traditional state regulation.

rules.title: Dormant Commerce Clause
rules.prompt_question: What is the dormant Commerce Clause rule?
rules.rule_statement: State and local laws may not discriminate against or unduly burden interstate commerce, even when Congress has not acted.
rules.application_example: A state law favoring in-state businesses over out-of-state competitors is suspect under the dormant Commerce Clause.
rules.common_trap: The dormant Commerce Clause limits states, not Congress.

rules.title: Discriminatory State Laws
rules.prompt_question: How are discriminatory state laws reviewed under the dormant Commerce Clause?
rules.rule_statement: A state law that discriminates against interstate commerce on its face, in purpose, or in practical effect is usually invalid unless the state proves it is necessary to achieve an important noneconomic purpose and no reasonable nondiscriminatory alternative exists.
rules.application_example: A state may not ban importation of out-of-state waste merely to protect local businesses.
rules.common_trap: Economic protectionism is almost always invalid.

rules.title: Nondiscriminatory Burdens on Interstate Commerce
rules.prompt_question: How are nondiscriminatory state laws reviewed under the dormant Commerce Clause?
rules.rule_statement: A nondiscriminatory law affecting interstate commerce is valid unless the burden on interstate commerce is clearly excessive in relation to the putative local benefits.
rules.application_example: A truck-length safety regulation may be upheld or struck down depending on the burden-benefit balance.
rules.common_trap: This is a balancing test, not strict scrutiny.

rules.title: Market Participant Exception
rules.prompt_question: What is the market participant exception?
rules.rule_statement: When a state acts as a market participant rather than a regulator, it may favor its own citizens in dealing with government-owned businesses or resources.
rules.application_example: A state-owned cement plant may prefer in-state buyers.
rules.common_trap: The exception does not allow downstream regulatory conditions unrelated to the immediate market participation.

rules.title: Article IV Privileges and Immunities Clause
rules.prompt_question: What does the Article IV Privileges and Immunities Clause prohibit?
rules.rule_statement: A state may not discriminate against out-of-state citizens with respect to fundamental rights or important economic activities unless the discrimination is closely related to a substantial state interest.
rules.application_example: A state may not charge nonresidents much higher licensing fees for practicing a common occupation without adequate justification.
rules.common_trap: Corporations cannot invoke Article IV privileges and immunities.

rules.title: Full Faith and Credit
rules.prompt_question: What does the Full Faith and Credit Clause require?
rules.rule_statement: States generally must recognize valid judgments from sister states if the rendering court had jurisdiction and the judgment is final and on the merits.
rules.application_example: A money judgment entered in one state is usually enforceable in another state.
rules.common_trap: A state need not apply another state's law in all circumstances, but judgments receive stronger protection.

rules.title: Treaty Power
rules.prompt_question: How are treaties made and what is their status?
rules.rule_statement: The President negotiates treaties, which become effective with approval by two-thirds of the Senate; valid treaties are supreme law, subject to the Constitution.
rules.application_example: A ratified treaty may preempt conflicting state law.
rules.common_trap: A treaty cannot override the Constitution.

rules.title: Executive Agreements
rules.prompt_question: What is the status of executive agreements?
rules.rule_statement: Executive agreements made by the President usually do not require Senate approval and generally prevail over conflicting state law, but they do not prevail over existing federal statutes or the Constitution.
rules.application_example: The President may enter certain foreign agreements without treaty ratification.
rules.common_trap: An executive agreement cannot override a federal statute enacted by Congress.

rules.title: Executive Privilege
rules.prompt_question: What is executive privilege?
rules.rule_statement: The President has a qualified privilege to withhold confidential presidential communications, especially in matters of national security or foreign affairs, but the privilege may yield to demonstrated need in criminal proceedings.
rules.application_example: A court may compel disclosure of presidential materials in a criminal case upon a sufficient showing of need.
rules.common_trap: The privilege is not absolute in ordinary criminal investigations.

rules.title: Presidential Immunity
rules.prompt_question: What immunity does the President have?
rules.rule_statement: The President has absolute immunity from civil damages liability for official acts, but no absolute immunity for unofficial conduct or from criminal process rules otherwise applicable.
rules.application_example: A damages suit based on a presidential policy decision is barred.
rules.common_trap: Official-act immunity is distinct from impeachment and criminal accountability questions.

rules.title: Appointments Clause
rules.prompt_question: How must federal officers be appointed?
rules.rule_statement: Principal officers must be appointed by the President with Senate confirmation, while Congress may vest appointment of inferior officers in the President alone, courts of law, or heads of departments.
rules.application_example: Cabinet secretaries require Senate confirmation.
rules.common_trap: Congress itself may not appoint executive officers.

rules.title: Removal of Executive Officers
rules.prompt_question: What is the President's removal power?
rules.rule_statement: The President generally may remove executive officers, but Congress may limit removal of certain inferior officers or members of independent regulatory commissions when such limits do not unduly interfere with executive power.
rules.application_example: Congress may provide good-cause protection for some independent agency members.
rules.common_trap: Congress may not reserve to itself the power to remove executive officers except through impeachment.

rules.title: Legislative Veto
rules.prompt_question: Is a legislative veto constitutional?
rules.rule_statement: A one-house or two-house legislative veto of executive action is unconstitutional because Congress must act through bicameralism and presentment unless the Constitution provides otherwise.
rules.application_example: Congress cannot reserve a one-house disapproval power over executive implementation of a statute.
rules.common_trap: Congress may still control through ordinary legislation.

rules.title: Impeachment
rules.prompt_question: How does impeachment work?
rules.rule_statement: The House has the sole power to impeach by majority vote, and the Senate tries impeachments and convicts by two-thirds vote; removal follows conviction.
rules.application_example: A federal judge may be removed only through impeachment and conviction.
rules.common_trap: Impeachment is a political process, not a criminal conviction.

rules.title: Nondelegation Doctrine
rules.prompt_question: When may Congress delegate power?
rules.rule_statement: Congress may delegate legislative authority if it supplies an intelligible principle to guide the exercise of delegated discretion.
rules.application_example: A statute authorizing an agency to regulate air pollutants to protect public health is usually upheld if standards guide the agency.
rules.common_trap: The doctrine is rarely used to strike statutes down on the MBE.

rules.title: State Action Requirement
rules.prompt_question: When do constitutional protections apply to private conduct?
rules.rule_statement: Constitutional protections generally apply only to governmental action, not purely private conduct, unless a recognized state action doctrine makes the private conduct fairly attributable to the state.
rules.application_example: A private club ordinarily is not bound by equal protection unless it performs a traditional exclusive public function or is entangled with the state.
rules.common_trap: Congress may regulate private conduct under statutes such as the Civil Rights Act, but that is different from direct constitutional application.

rules.title: Public Function and Entanglement
rules.prompt_question: When is private conduct treated as state action?
rules.rule_statement: Private conduct may be treated as state action if the private actor performs a traditionally exclusive public function or there is significant state entanglement, coercion, or encouragement.
rules.application_example: A company operating a town may be treated as a state actor for some constitutional purposes.
rules.common_trap: Mere licensing or regulation is usually insufficient.

rules.title: Content-Based Restrictions
rules.prompt_question: How are content-based speech restrictions reviewed?
rules.rule_statement: A law that regulates speech based on content is subject to strict scrutiny and must be narrowly tailored to serve a compelling governmental interest.
rules.application_example: A law banning speech criticizing the government is content based and presumptively invalid.
rules.common_trap: Viewpoint discrimination is an especially disfavored form of content discrimination.

rules.title: Viewpoint Discrimination
rules.prompt_question: What is viewpoint discrimination?
rules.rule_statement: Viewpoint discrimination occurs when the government allows speech on a subject but suppresses one side of the debate, and such discrimination is almost always unconstitutional.
rules.application_example: A university cannot fund student speech supporting environmentalism while denying funding to opposing views because of disagreement with the viewpoint.
rules.common_trap: This is usually invalid even in limited public forums if the exclusion is viewpoint based.

rules.title: Content-Neutral Time, Place, and Manner Restrictions
rules.prompt_question: When are content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions valid?
rules.rule_statement: A content-neutral regulation of speech in a public forum is valid if it is narrowly tailored to serve an important governmental interest and leaves open ample alternative channels of communication.
rules.application_example: A permit requirement for parades may be valid if administered under objective standards.
rules.common_trap: Narrow tailoring here does not require the least restrictive means.

rules.title: Traditional Public Forums
rules.prompt_question: What is a traditional public forum?
rules.rule_statement: Streets, sidewalks, and parks are traditional public forums where speech restrictions receive the greatest protection.
rules.application_example: Speech in a city park is ordinarily protected against content-based restrictions.
rules.common_trap: Not all government property is a public forum.

rules.title: Designated Public Forums
rules.prompt_question: What is a designated public forum?
rules.rule_statement: A designated public forum is government property intentionally opened for expressive activity, and speech restrictions there are reviewed similarly to those in a traditional public forum.
rules.application_example: A municipal theater opened for public debate may be treated as a designated public forum.
rules.common_trap: The government may close a designated forum prospectively so long as it does not discriminate unconstitutionally.

rules.title: Limited and Nonpublic Forums
rules.prompt_question: How are speech restrictions reviewed in limited and nonpublic forums?
rules.rule_statement: In a limited or nonpublic forum, the government may impose restrictions that are reasonable and viewpoint neutral.
rules.application_example: A school board meeting limited to agenda items may exclude unrelated speech if done reasonably and without viewpoint discrimination.
rules.common_trap: Reasonableness review still forbids viewpoint discrimination.

rules.title: Prior Restraints
rules.prompt_question: What is the rule on prior restraints?
rules.rule_statement: Prior restraints on speech are heavily disfavored and generally unconstitutional unless accompanied by important procedural safeguards and supported by an extraordinary justification.
rules.application_example: A licensing scheme allowing officials unbridled discretion to deny publication is invalid.
rules.common_trap: Prior restraints are usually worse than subsequent punishment.

rules.title: Overbreadth
rules.prompt_question: What is the overbreadth doctrine?
rules.rule_statement: A law regulating speech is facially invalid if it prohibits a substantial amount of protected speech relative to its plainly legitimate sweep.
rules.application_example: A broad ban on annoying speech may be struck down because it covers protected expression.
rules.common_trap: Overbreadth is a special First Amendment doctrine allowing facial challenges.

rules.title: Vagueness
rules.prompt_question: When is a speech regulation void for vagueness?
rules.rule_statement: A law is void for vagueness if it fails to give reasonable notice of what is prohibited or invites arbitrary and discriminatory enforcement.
rules.application_example: A prohibition on speech that is 'improper' without clear standards may be void.
rules.common_trap: Vagueness concerns fair notice and enforcement discretion.

rules.title: Symbolic Conduct
rules.prompt_question: When is expressive conduct protected speech?
rules.rule_statement: Expressive conduct is protected when there is an intent to convey a message likely to be understood, and government regulation unrelated to suppression of expression is valid if it furthers an important interest and is no greater than essential.
rules.application_example: Burning a draft card may be regulable because the law protects the government's administrative interests unrelated to expression.
rules.common_trap: Flag burning is protected because the regulation there targets expressive impact.

rules.title: Incitement
rules.prompt_question: When may speech be punished as incitement?
rules.rule_statement: Advocacy is unprotected incitement only if it is directed to producing imminent lawless action and is likely to produce such action.
rules.application_example: Abstract advocacy of violence at some future time is protected, but a direct call to attack immediately may be punished.
rules.common_trap: The imminence requirement is strict.

rules.title: Fighting Words
rules.prompt_question: What are fighting words?
rules.rule_statement: Fighting words are personally abusive epithets inherently likely to provoke an immediate violent reaction, and they may be regulated in narrow circumstances.
rules.application_example: A face-to-face personal insult provoking a breach of the peace may qualify.
rules.common_trap: The category is narrow and not all offensive speech counts.

rules.title: True Threats
rules.prompt_question: What is a true threat?
rules.rule_statement: A true threat is a serious expression of intent to commit unlawful violence against a person or group, and such threats are unprotected by the First Amendment.
rules.application_example: Sending a message seriously threatening to shoot a judge may be punished.
rules.common_trap: Political hyperbole is usually protected if not a serious threat.

rules.title: Obscenity
rules.prompt_question: What is the Miller test for obscenity?
rules.rule_statement: Material is obscene if the average person applying contemporary community standards would find it appeals to the prurient interest, it depicts sexual conduct in a patently offensive way as defined by law, and taken as a whole it lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value.
rules.application_example: Hardcore sexual material may be obscene if it satisfies all three elements of the Miller test.
rules.common_trap: Serious value is judged more objectively and can save material from being obscene.

rules.title: Child Pornography
rules.prompt_question: How is child pornography treated under the First Amendment?
rules.rule_statement: Child pornography involving actual minors may be prohibited regardless of whether it is obscene, because the government has a compelling interest in protecting children from exploitation.
rules.application_example: Possession or distribution of visual depictions involving actual minors in sexual conduct may be criminalized.
rules.common_trap: Virtual depictions not involving actual minors receive greater protection.

rules.title: Commercial Speech
rules.prompt_question: How is commercial speech reviewed?
rules.rule_statement: Truthful, lawful commercial speech may be regulated only if the government has a substantial interest, the regulation directly advances that interest, and the regulation is not more extensive than necessary.
rules.application_example: A ban on misleading attorney advertising may be valid, but a broad ban on truthful price advertising may fail.
rules.common_trap: False or misleading commercial speech receives no First Amendment protection.

rules.title: Defamation of Public Officials and Figures
rules.prompt_question: What must a public official or public figure prove in a defamation action?
rules.rule_statement: A public official or public figure must prove falsity and actual malice, meaning knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for truth, by clear and convincing evidence.
rules.application_example: A mayor suing a newspaper must prove actual malice to recover for defamation.
rules.common_trap: Actual malice concerns awareness of falsity, not hostility.

rules.title: Anonymous Speech
rules.prompt_question: Is anonymous speech protected?
rules.rule_statement: Anonymous speech is generally protected by the First Amendment, subject to ordinary valid content-neutral regulations and other applicable constitutional limits.
rules.application_example: A state may not broadly ban anonymous campaign literature without strong justification.
rules.common_trap: Disclosure requirements in election law sometimes receive different analysis.

rules.title: Free Exercise and Neutral Laws
rules.prompt_question: How are neutral, generally applicable laws treated under the Free Exercise Clause?
rules.rule_statement: A neutral law of general applicability that incidentally burdens religion usually does not violate the Free Exercise Clause and is reviewed under rational basis, not strict scrutiny.
rules.application_example: A generally applicable drug law may be enforced against religious use of controlled substances.
rules.common_trap: If the law is not neutral or not generally applicable, stricter review applies.

rules.title: Laws Targeting Religion
rules.prompt_question: How are laws targeting religion reviewed?
rules.rule_statement: A law that targets religion, discriminates among religions, or creates a system of individualized exemptions hostile to religion is subject to strict scrutiny.
rules.application_example: A city ordinance aimed specifically at suppressing a religious practice is presumptively invalid.
rules.common_trap: Look for hostility or underinclusiveness revealing religious targeting.

rules.title: Establishment Clause General Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the general rule under the Establishment Clause?
rules.rule_statement: The government may not establish religion, favor one religion over another, or unduly entangle itself with religion.
rules.application_example: A law preferring one denomination over others is unconstitutional.
rules.common_trap: Modern cases focus heavily on historical practice and neutrality depending on context.

rules.title: Religion in Public Schools
rules.prompt_question: What are the basic Establishment Clause rules for religion in public schools?
rules.rule_statement: Government-sponsored prayer, devotional Bible reading, and religious exercises in public schools are generally unconstitutional, while neutral accommodation and private student religious expression are treated differently.
rules.application_example: A public school may not lead students in prayer at graduation.
rules.common_trap: Private student speech is not automatically state action.

rules.title: Ministerial Exception
rules.prompt_question: What is the ministerial exception?
rules.rule_statement: The Religion Clauses bar many employment-discrimination suits by ministers or similar key religious personnel against their religious institutions concerning hiring and firing decisions.
rules.application_example: A church-school teacher performing significant religious functions may fall within the ministerial exception.
rules.common_trap: The exception turns on function, not merely title.

rules.title: Second Amendment
rules.prompt_question: What does the Second Amendment protect?
rules.rule_statement: The Second Amendment protects an individual's right to keep and bear arms for lawful purposes such as self-defense, subject to historical and traditionally accepted regulations.
rules.application_example: A total ban on handgun possession in the home is unconstitutional.
rules.common_trap: The right is not unlimited and longstanding regulations may remain valid.

rules.title: Fundamental Rights and Strict Scrutiny
rules.prompt_question: How are laws burdening fundamental rights reviewed under substantive due process?
rules.rule_statement: A law that substantially burdens a fundamental right is subject to strict scrutiny and must be narrowly tailored to serve a compelling governmental interest.
rules.application_example: A law severely restricting parental control over child upbringing would be tested under strict scrutiny.
rules.common_trap: Only rights deeply rooted in the nation's history and tradition or otherwise recognized as fundamental receive this treatment.

rules.title: Right to Travel
rules.prompt_question: What constitutional protection applies to the right to travel?
rules.rule_statement: The Constitution protects the right of interstate travel, including the right to enter and leave states and to be treated equally as a new resident regarding fundamental matters.
rules.application_example: A law denying welfare benefits to recent arrivals may burden the right to travel.
rules.common_trap: Durational residency requirements often trigger strict scrutiny when affecting basic benefits or voting.

rules.title: Right to Vote
rules.prompt_question: How are burdens on voting rights reviewed?
rules.rule_statement: Severe burdens on the right to vote are subject to strict scrutiny, while reasonable, nondiscriminatory election regulations are reviewed under a more balancing-oriented standard.
rules.application_example: A poll tax in state elections is unconstitutional.
rules.common_trap: Not every election regulation triggers strict scrutiny.

rules.title: Ballot Access
rules.prompt_question: How are ballot access restrictions reviewed?
rules.rule_statement: Ballot access restrictions are evaluated by balancing the character and magnitude of the burden against the state's justifications, with severe burdens receiving more exacting scrutiny.
rules.application_example: A state may impose reasonable signature requirements but not unduly exclusionary ones.
rules.common_trap: This is not always classic strict scrutiny language.

rules.title: One Person, One Vote
rules.prompt_question: What is the one person, one vote principle?
rules.rule_statement: Legislative voting districts within a state must be substantially equal in population so that each person's vote carries roughly equal weight.
rules.application_example: A state legislative plan with major population disparities is unconstitutional absent valid justification.
rules.common_trap: Congressional districts require near mathematical equality; state and local districts allow slightly more flexibility.

rules.title: Partisan Gerrymandering
rules.prompt_question: Are partisan gerrymandering claims justiciable in federal court?
rules.rule_statement: Claims of partisan gerrymandering present nonjusticiable political questions in federal court.
rules.application_example: A federal court will not adjudicate a purely partisan-gerrymandering challenge.
rules.common_trap: Racial gerrymandering is different and is justiciable.

rules.title: Racial Gerrymandering
rules.prompt_question: How is racial gerrymandering reviewed?
rules.rule_statement: If race was the predominant factor in drawing district lines, the districting plan is subject to strict scrutiny.
rules.application_example: A bizarrely shaped district drawn mainly to separate voters by race may be unconstitutional unless narrowly tailored to a compelling interest.
rules.common_trap: Compliance with the Voting Rights Act can be compelling, but the means must still be narrowly tailored.

rules.title: Equal Protection Framework
rules.prompt_question: What is the basic equal protection framework?
rules.rule_statement: Equal protection analysis asks whether the government has drawn a classification and, if so, applies rational basis, intermediate scrutiny, or strict scrutiny depending on the classification or right burdened.
rules.application_example: A law classifying on the basis of sex receives intermediate scrutiny.
rules.common_trap: Federal equal protection claims arise through the Fifth Amendment Due Process Clause, not the Fourteenth Amendment directly.

rules.title: Rational Basis Review
rules.prompt_question: What is rational basis review?
rules.rule_statement: A law is upheld under rational basis review if it is rationally related to a legitimate governmental interest.
rules.application_example: A state may distinguish between licensed and unlicensed occupations if the distinction is rational.
rules.common_trap: The challenger almost always loses under ordinary rational basis review.

rules.title: Strict Scrutiny
rules.prompt_question: What is strict scrutiny?
rules.rule_statement: Under strict scrutiny, the government must show the law is narrowly tailored to achieve a compelling governmental interest.
rules.application_example: A racial classification favoring one group over another is presumptively invalid unless strict scrutiny is satisfied.
rules.common_trap: This is the toughest standard and is often fatal in fact.

rules.title: Intermediate Scrutiny
rules.prompt_question: What is intermediate scrutiny?
rules.rule_statement: Under intermediate scrutiny, the government must show the classification is substantially related to an important governmental interest.
rules.application_example: Most sex-based classifications receive intermediate scrutiny.
rules.common_trap: For sex classifications, the government must offer an exceedingly persuasive justification.

rules.title: Suspect Classifications
rules.prompt_question: Which classifications are suspect?
rules.rule_statement: Race, national origin, and classifications affecting some noncitizens are suspect and generally trigger strict scrutiny.
rules.application_example: A state law denying a professional license to lawful permanent residents usually triggers strict scrutiny.
rules.common_trap: Federal alienage classifications generally receive only rational basis because of federal plenary power over immigration.

rules.title: Sex Classifications
rules.prompt_question: How are sex classifications reviewed?
rules.rule_statement: Sex classifications are subject to intermediate scrutiny and require an exceedingly persuasive justification.
rules.application_example: A law granting benefits to women but not similarly situated men is presumptively invalid unless substantially related to an important interest.
rules.common_trap: Administrative convenience is not enough.

rules.title: Nonmarital Children
rules.prompt_question: How are classifications based on legitimacy reviewed?
rules.rule_statement: Classifications disadvantaging nonmarital children are subject to intermediate scrutiny.
rules.application_example: A law denying inheritance rights to some nonmarital children may fail if not substantially related to an important interest.
rules.common_trap: The Court is skeptical of punishing children for parents' conduct.

rules.title: State Alienage Classifications
rules.prompt_question: How are state alienage classifications reviewed?
rules.rule_statement: State classifications based on alienage usually receive strict scrutiny, except for classifications concerning democratic self-government and political function positions, which receive rational basis.
rules.application_example: A state may require police officers or public school teachers to be citizens under the political-function exception in some circumstances.
rules.common_trap: Federal alienage rules are treated differently.

rules.title: Wealth and Education
rules.prompt_question: Are poverty and education suspect classifications or fundamental rights?
rules.rule_statement: Wealth is generally not a suspect classification and education is not a fundamental right, so ordinary distinctions in school funding usually receive rational basis review.
rules.application_example: A school-finance challenge ordinarily receives rational basis review absent some other fundamental-right burden.
rules.common_trap: Access-to-courts cases involving filing fees can be different when fundamental interests are implicated.

rules.title: Procedural Due Process
rules.prompt_question: When does procedural due process apply?
rules.rule_statement: Procedural due process applies when the government deprives a person of life, liberty, or property, and requires notice and an opportunity to be heard at a meaningful time and in a meaningful manner.
rules.application_example: Before terminating welfare benefits, the government generally must provide some form of hearing.
rules.common_trap: The plaintiff must first identify a protected liberty or property interest.

rules.title: Property Interests
rules.prompt_question: What is a property interest for procedural due process purposes?
rules.rule_statement: A property interest exists when a person has a legitimate claim of entitlement created by law, contract, or established practice, not merely a unilateral expectation.
rules.application_example: A public employee with for-cause job protection has a property interest in continued employment.
rules.common_trap: At-will government employees usually lack such a property interest.

rules.title: Liberty Interests
rules.prompt_question: What is a liberty interest for procedural due process purposes?
rules.rule_statement: Liberty interests include freedom from bodily restraint and certain important reputational or status harms when combined with a change in legal status, as well as some interests created by law.
rules.application_example: A prisoner facing atypical and significant hardship may have a protected liberty interest.
rules.common_trap: Reputation alone is usually not enough without a plus factor.

rules.title: Mathews Balancing Test
rules.prompt_question: How is the amount of process due determined?
rules.rule_statement: Courts balance the private interest affected, the risk of erroneous deprivation and value of additional safeguards, and the government's interest including administrative burden.
rules.application_example: A court may require more process before terminating disability benefits than before imposing a brief school suspension.
rules.common_trap: The process due can vary by context.

rules.title: Takings Clause General Rule
rules.prompt_question: What does the Takings Clause provide?
rules.rule_statement: Private property may not be taken for public use without just compensation.
rules.application_example: If the government condemns land to build a highway, it must pay fair compensation.
rules.common_trap: The Takings Clause applies to states through the Fourteenth Amendment.

rules.title: Physical Taking
rules.prompt_question: When is there a physical taking?
rules.rule_statement: A permanent physical occupation authorized by the government is a taking requiring just compensation, no matter how small the occupation.
rules.application_example: A law requiring landlords to allow permanent cable boxes on buildings effects a taking.
rules.common_trap: Permanent physical occupation triggers a per se rule.

rules.title: Total Regulatory Taking
rules.prompt_question: When does a regulation effect a total taking?
rules.rule_statement: A regulation that deprives land of all economically viable use is a taking unless the restricted use was already prohibited by background principles of state property or nuisance law.
rules.application_example: A regulation forbidding any productive use of land may require compensation.
rules.common_trap: If any economically viable use remains, the total-taking rule does not apply.

rules.title: Partial Regulatory Taking
rules.prompt_question: How are partial regulatory takings analyzed?
rules.rule_statement: If a regulation does not eliminate all economic value, courts consider economic impact, interference with investment-backed expectations, and the character of the governmental action.
rules.application_example: A zoning law reducing development value may or may not be a taking depending on the Penn Central factors.
rules.common_trap: Diminution in value alone is usually not enough.

rules.title: Public Use
rules.prompt_question: What counts as public use?
rules.rule_statement: Public use is broadly construed to include a public purpose, such as economic development or elimination of blight, so long as the taking is rationally related to that purpose.
rules.application_example: Taking property as part of a comprehensive redevelopment plan may satisfy public use.
rules.common_trap: Public use does not require the public to own or directly access the property after the taking.

rules.title: Just Compensation
rules.prompt_question: What is just compensation?
rules.rule_statement: Just compensation is usually the fair market value of the property at the time of the taking.
rules.application_example: The owner is entitled to the property's objective market value, not sentimental value.
rules.common_trap: Consequential damages are generally not included unless separately provided by law.

rules.title: Incorporated Bill of Rights
rules.prompt_question: How do many criminal procedure protections apply to the states?
rules.rule_statement: Most provisions of the Bill of Rights protecting individual liberties are incorporated against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause.
rules.application_example: The First Amendment and most criminal procedure protections constrain state governments as well as the federal government.
rules.common_trap: A few provisions are not fully incorporated.

rules.title: Contracts Clause
rules.prompt_question: What does the Contracts Clause prohibit?
rules.rule_statement: States may not substantially impair existing contractual obligations unless the law is reasonably and appropriately tailored to serve an important public purpose.
rules.application_example: A state law rewriting private contract terms may be unconstitutional if the impairment is severe and insufficiently justified.
rules.common_trap: The Contracts Clause applies only to states, not the federal government.

rules.title: Ex Post Facto Laws and Bills of Attainder
rules.prompt_question: What are ex post facto laws and bills of attainder?
rules.rule_statement: An ex post facto law retroactively imposes criminal punishment or increases punishment for past conduct, and a bill of attainder legislatively inflicts punishment on named persons or an identifiable group without a judicial trial; both are prohibited.
rules.application_example: A statute increasing the sentence for a crime already committed is an ex post facto law.
rules.common_trap: Ex post facto limits apply to criminal or punitive laws, not ordinary civil regulation.
